name: ZAYTUNA COLLEGE
mission: Zaytuna College aims to educate and prepare morally committed professional, intellectual, and spiritual leaders, who are grounded in the Islamic scholar...
programs: BA in Liberal and Islamic Studies
ZCELL (Center for Ethical Living and Learning)
theoryOfChange: Zaytuna College describes its mission to educate and prepare morally committed professional, intellectual, and spiritual leaders grounded in the Islamic scholarly tradition and conversant with cultural currents and critical ideas shaping modern society; it also describes giving as 'the gift of knowledge' (sadaqah jariya) that will benefit humanity for generations, and states donations help students graduate debt-free.
scoreSummary: ZAYTUNA COLLEGE shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (major risk deductions applied).

evaluationSources: Accreditation (Western Association of Schools and Colleges (WASC))

zakatClaimEvidence: According to many scholars, institutions such as Zaytuna College meet the requirements for zakat eligibility. However, we have chosen to limit the zakat funds only to the total cost of education for zakat eligible students and not for any other institutional expenditures. We take this sacred trust very seriously, so if you choose to give a portion of your zakat to Zaytuna College, we will do our utmost, God willing, to ensure its most judicious and shariah compliant use. Additionally, Zaytuna College has a dedicated 'Zakat Fund' which 'serves as the basis of our financial aid program and is utilized to cover the total cost of educating a zakat-eligible student.' (Source: https://zaytuna.edu/Zakat-Essentials)

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Context dependent profile. Consider cause fit, risk profile, and evidence maturity before deciding.

keyConcerns.headline: Zakat-collecting organization holds 34 months of reserves
keyConcerns.detail: Zakat must be distributed promptly per Islamic jurisprudence. Large reserves raise concerns about timely distribution.

keyConcerns.headline: Fundraising costs are 28% of total expenses
keyConcerns.detail: High fundraising spend reduces funds available for programs.

components.evidence: Explicit zakat program (+4); Muslim-focused organization (+2); Islamic identity (+1)
components.improvement_suggestion: Clarify zakat allocation policy and eligible use categories for donors.
components.name: Muslim Donor Fit

alignment.rationale: Alignment 29/50: MEDIUM Muslim donor fit, education global cause area

components.improvement_suggestion: Publish beneficiary counts consistently so cost-effectiveness can be assessed.
components.name: Cost Per Beneficiary

components.improvement_suggestion: Publish a time-bound plan to deploy excess unrestricted reserves into mission delivery, while keeping a clear operating reserve floor.
components.name: Financial Health

judge_issues.message: The rationale mentions "moderate overall score" which is not quite accurate for a score of 49. It would be more accurate to describe it as slightly below average.

The impact rationale mentions "high data confidence level" which is not a factor in the impact score itself. It would be more accurate to focus on the program expense ratio and lack of cost-per-beneficiary data.

The risk summary mentions "zakat compliance (major risk deductions applied)". It would be more accurate to say that the risk deductions are related to financial risks, specifically the high reserves and fundraising costs, rather than a lack of zakat compliance.
Website citation for 'founded_year' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred
Website citation for 'has_audited_financials' uses homepage URL; deep link preferred

score_details.score_summary: ZAYTUNA COLLEGE shows good impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance (major risk deductions applied).

zakat.claim_evidence: According to many scholars, institutions such as Zaytuna College meet the requirements for zakat eligibility. However, we have chosen to limit the zakat funds only to the total cost of education for zakat eligible students and not for any other institutional expenditures. We take this sacred trust very seriously, so if you choose to give a portion of your zakat to Zaytuna College, we will do our utmost, God willing, to ensure its most judicious and shariah compliant use. Additionally, Zaytuna College has a dedicated 'Zakat Fund' which 'serves as the basis of our financial aid program and is utilized to cover the total cost of educating a zakat-eligible student.' (Source: https://zaytuna.edu/Zakat-Essentials)

all_citations.claim: The college reported total revenue of $18,021,649, a program expense ratio of 56.0%, working capital of 33.6 months, and fundraising efficiency of $0.12 per $1 raised.
Zaytuna College is zakat-eligible and maintains specific policies regarding zakat essentials.
Financial data including revenue and expenses are documented in the 2023 Form 990.
The college tracks impact through degrees awarded and digital views.
Zaytuna offers a Bachelor of Arts in Liberal Arts and Islamic Studies and is an accredited institution.
The college has an external zakat policy for determining eligibility.
Zakat eligibility determination is part of the audited financial oversight.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: Zaytuna College earns a moderate overall score due to its unique educational niche and strong financial liquidity, balanced against a program expense ratio that suggests significant spending on administrative or fundraising overhead.
areas_for_improvement: The program expense ratio of 56.0% is relatively low compared to peer educational institutions [1].
Direct impact data regarding cost per beneficiary is currently unavailable in public reporting [4].

dimension_explanations.alignment: With an alignment score of 29/50, the college serves a medium donor fit by addressing the urgent global need for Islamic higher education [5]. The institution is verified as zakat-eligible, following specific internal policies to ensure funds are distributed to eligible asnaf categories [2][8].
dimension_explanations.impact: The college demonstrates direct service through the awarding of degrees and digital educational outreach, though its impact score of 29/50 reflects a lack of specific cost-per-beneficiary data [4][5]. While the institution maintains a high data confidence level, its program expense ratio is 56.0%, indicating that a portion of its $18,021,649 total revenue is directed toward non-programmatic costs [1][3].

baseline_narrative.headline: Zaytuna College is the first accredited Muslim liberal arts college in the United States, dedicated to grounding students in both Islamic and Western scholarly traditions.
strengths: Maintains a robust working capital of 33.6 months, ensuring long-term institutional stability [1].
High fundraising efficiency with a cost of only $0.12 per $1 raised [1].
Established zakat policy that allows donors to support eligible students through religious giving [7].
baseline_narrative.summary: Located in Berkeley, California, Zaytuna College offers specialized degree programs including a Bachelor of Arts in Liberal Arts and Islamic Studies and a Master of Arts in Islamic Texts [5]. The institution reported a total revenue of $18,021,649 and maintains a zakat-eligible status to support its educational mission [1][2].

alignment.explanation: The college is a <cite id="2">strong fit for Muslim donors</cite> due to its <cite id="2">explicit zakat policy</cite> and focus on <cite id="5">Islamic scholarly traditions</cite>.
alignment.improvement: Clarifying the <cite id="2">zakat allocation policy</cite> and specific use categories would further strengthen donor alignment.

credibility.explanation: Zaytuna maintains high credibility through <cite id="5">WASC accreditation</cite> and a <cite id="3">Gold Seal of Transparency from Candid</cite>. Governance is managed by an <cite id="3">independent board of 12 members</cite>.
credibility.improvement: To further enhance credibility, the college could <cite id="4">publish consistent beneficiary counts</cite> to allow for better cost-effectiveness analysis.

impact.explanation: Impact is delivered through <cite id="4">direct degree-granting programs</cite> and <cite id="6">community service requirements</cite>. However, the <cite id="3">program expense ratio of 56.0%</cite> suggests room for more aggressive spending on student services.

rich_narrative.headline: Zaytuna College is the first accredited Muslim liberal arts college in the United States, dedicated to grounding students in both Islamic and Western scholarly traditions.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who want to invest in the long-term intellectual infrastructure of the American Muslim community through an accredited educational institution.
donor_motivations: Want to support the first accredited Muslim college in the US
Value the preservation of Islamic scholarly traditions in the West
Prefer giving to institutions with high financial stability and low risk of closure
Seek to fulfill zakat through student scholarships
giving_considerations: Donations contribute to an institution with very high cash reserves
Program spending is currently lower than many humanitarian peers
Focus is on long-term leadership development rather than immediate relief
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking immediate humanitarian impact or those who prefer charities with high program-to-overhead ratios (85%+).

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade C reflects verified academic outcomes and accreditation, but lacks independent cost-per-beneficiary evaluations.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: Zaytuna creates impact by training morally committed leaders through a dual curriculum of Islamic and Liberal Arts, validated by external accreditation.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Accreditation ensures that the degrees awarded have recognized value in the professional and academic world.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: While the college has high reserves, its 19.7% growth rate suggests active expansion of programs and faculty.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Zaytuna is the <cite id="5">only accredited Muslim undergraduate college</cite> in the US, giving it a unique niche compared to graduate-only peers.

strengths.detail: The college maintains <cite id="1">33.6 months of working capital</cite>, providing a massive buffer for long-term institutional survival.
Zaytuna spends only <cite id="1">$0.11 to raise every $1</cite>, which is significantly better than industry standards.
The institution is <cite id="5">accredited by the WASC Senior College and University Commission</cite>, validating its academic rigor.

strengths_deep_dive: Zaytuna College has demonstrated a strong ability to scale its resources, achieving a <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of 19.7%</cite>. This growth is supported by a <cite id="3">diversified revenue stream</cite> that includes <cite id="3">$15.2 million in contributions</cite> and <cite id="3">$2.3 million in program service revenue</cite>. Their <cite id="4">digital reach is extensive</cite>, with <cite id="4">36 million digital views</cite> reported, extending their educational impact far beyond the physical campus in Berkeley.
rich_narrative.summary: founded in 2009, Zaytuna College stands as the <cite id="5">first accredited Muslim liberal arts college</cite> in the United States. Based in Berkeley, California, the institution seeks to bridge the gap between Islamic and Western scholarly traditions through its <cite id="5">BA in Liberal Arts and Islamic Studies</cite> and <cite id="6">MA in Islamic Texts</cite>. The college operates as a <cite id="4">direct service provider</cite> in the higher education space, having <cite id="4">awarded 158 degrees</cite> to date. Financially, the organization reported a <cite id="3">total revenue of $18,359,667</cite> in 2023, reflecting a <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of 19.7%</cite>. While the college maintains <cite id="1">extraordinary financial stability</cite> with <cite id="1">33.6 months of working capital</cite>, its <cite id="3">program expense ratio of 56.0%</cite> is notably lower than the <cite id="3">peer median of 85.3%</cite>. This indicates that a significant portion of annual revenue is directed toward institutional growth and reserves rather than immediate educational delivery. For Muslim donors, the college offers a <cite id="2">verified zakat-eligible giving path</cite>, specifically designed to support students who qualify under categories such as <cite id="2">the poor (fuqara) and the needy (masakin)</cite>.
